All right, we're back to your next lesson on looping and here's the deal. If you haven't done your exercises, not just the quiz, but the exercises, I want you to stop right now and go back and do them. This is the gift you give yourself. These exercises with looping are crucial because looping, while it is an art, it's so scientifically organized. Such an incredible syntax, one part building on the next. So if it's kind of one little chink in the arm, well, it still works. But I promise you this, if you do this right, you will quickly get to the point where you can close anyone who's closable. So please do your exercises right now if you haven't stopped and I'll wait for a second. Okay, I guess you have, right? So let's roll forward. We'll pick up right where we left off. So you had this language pattern where you combined just the right words that create a scream from the hilltop airtight logical case, right? And you said those words using that really powerful tonality of pace, pace lead so that when you ended that pattern and you say, you see what I'm saying here, Bill? You like the idea and Bill says, Oh yeah, yeah, of course I do. Because Bill will actually match your level of enthusiasm and certainty because of pace, pace lead. So essentially what happens here is your words that scream from the hilltops airtight logical case with its assertions and its, you know, just perfect coherence, mathematically, logically, logistically, the whole millionaires, the benefits, the whole everything, right? You do all of that, which moves to a very high level of logical certainty while pace, pace lead goes to work on the emotional certainty, right? And boom, now you've done those two crucial things. A, you moved them logically and B, you moved them also emotionally in one swift swoop. So let's slow things down now for a second. I want to ask you a really important question. See what's happened now is because of this last pattern that you ran, you've now moved Bill, it's called Bill, right? So a very high level of logical certainty, probably nine or above. And on the emotional certainty scale, he's probably, you know, seven and a half, maybe an eight depending on where he started, right? So here's my question. Would it make sense to ask him for the order right now? Try to get him to buy from you because remember, if he's got a very low action threshold, maybe you'll slide it under that radar. So would you ask him to buy? Yes or no? Correct. Of course you wouldn't. It would be an exercise in futility. And here's why. No, in fact, you actually wouldn't. It would be an exercise in futility, as the phrase goes. And let me tell you exactly why. You see, here's the deal. There's not one 10. There are three 10s. And all you've done so far is address the first 10. Understand, we have to be at a really high level of certainty for all three of the three 10s. Remember, back to logic one of you, one of those is missing. You basically have no shot of closing at all. So there's really no justification. Think about this. Why would Bill or Mary or Jane, why would he or she trust you right now at a level that was high enough to make them feel comfortable enough to buy? I mean, yes. There's some rapport that's been built simply by your active listening and asking questions. But I'm told that at high level of trust from knowing someone or from really understanding why someone is trustworthy, that doesn't exist right now. So there's no justification. Now, what will not happen is the prospect won't say, well, I'm sorry, Jordan, you shouldn't have done that because you haven't addressed my second 10 yet. I'm not going to say that, right? What they'll say is, oh, it sounds really good, but and they'll hop to a new objection. Notice what happens here. If you ask for the order and you only have the first 10 lined up, all that will happen is it will cause the prospect to start focusing on the second and third 10s. You're not going to close the deal unless it's got an ultra low action threshold, the prospect, right? Or they entered in a really, really high level, which is incredibly rare. So it's just not worth the risk at this point. It doesn't make sense to do. In fact, let's go back to the introduction of this module for a moment. Remember I said about looping? I said the definition of a loop. It starts when you get hit with an objection and essentially you circle back and you create more certainty for all three of the three 10s. And then sometimes you might even lower the action threshold in your later loops and on pain, but certainly you're always going to be touching all three 10s. So that first pattern wasn't an entire loop. It was the first pattern within your first loop. There's a difference. In other words, what you've done so far in this particular loop, your first loop, is you've raised the level of logical certainty for the first 10 to a very high level. You've also gone to work on the emotional component of the first 10. Raise that to a higher level, right? So guess what? We have work to do. We have to also raise the prospects level of certainty for both the second and the third 10. And think about this. What we're really saying is as follows. You have to convince someone that you've just met for the first time maybe 10 or 15 minutes ago, maybe even talked about the phone, even seen it in person. You have to convince this person to trust you at a really, really high level. Also trust your company as well right in the next minute or two or you're done. That's what you got to do. Sounds like a daunting task, right? I mean to create that level of trust from someone that quickly. We never met you before. Think about that, right? Sounds tough, right? Well guess what? It's actually pretty easy thanks to a really famous person from the movies. All famous in fact. So what you probably love and are familiar with, let's you know what North Korea that is, right? But what I'm talking about here is that ping-pong playing, shrimp eating, Jenny loving, premature jacketing, person named Forest Gump, whose five-year-old incarnation is the entity or the person who I used as the inspiration to create this language pattern, my Forest Gump pattern. Now I want to keep these lessons short here in the looping section. So I'm just going to tell you the story real quick and then we'll cut to a quiz. So very quickly here, the story is from the movie Forest Gump. Remember when Forest was five years old going to his first day of school and his mommy takes him up to the bus, right? And he's there staring at the space like Forest typically does right off the, like nothing, right? The bus pulls up, door opens, the driver on gruff looking with a cigarette dangling out of her mouth. She looks down, Forest looks up, he freezes like a deer in the headlights. Doesn't know what to do. The driver goes, are you getting on? He goes, my mom had told me not to take rides from strangers. The driver is like, what? She goes, well, I'm your bus driver. He goes, I can't get on the bus with you. Just add them here, right? And she starts to realize what she's dealing with. So she softens her toe to bit. She goes, well, this is the bus to school. You can get on. Forest though is not moved. See what's happening here. He doesn't know this person, so has no basis for trusting her. So there's a deadlock. She's looking at him. He's looking at her frozen. Finally an inspiration comes over Forest. He says, I'm Forest, Forest, go. I'm Dorothy Harry. Well, now we ain't strangers anymore. And just like that, Forest gets on the bus. And that is how simple things are. And again, I know this is a movie. It's not real life. But here's the deal. When it comes to trust, human beings are built in this really odd way. See, we don't know somebody. We tend to distrust them actually more than we should. We might have this intense distrust depending on the person. Yet once that pendulum of trust starts to swing, it swings wildly. It swings violently all the way to the end. And all the sons who didn't trust you five seconds ago was like your best friend. Once we make that decision, bam. We make that decision fully and wholly. The key here is how do you get that pendulum to swing? And that's what the next lesson is all about. So let's cut now to a quiz. We got to stop here. I want you to please lock all this stuff in and come right back if you can. And I will see you in the next lesson.
